explicit_text: I was born on a farm east of Tonkawa, Oklahoma, though my family always lived there. The land my father owned was about 10 miles north of where we live now, near Tonkawa. It’s a place that marks the beginning of my story and my family's roots in Oklahoma.
implicit_text: I was born on a farm east of a small city in northern Oklahoma, though my family always lived there. The land my father owned was about 10 miles north of where we live now, near that city. It’s a place that marks the beginning of my story and my family's roots in Oklahoma.
cues: born on farm east of Tonkawa

explicit_text: Our home place is seven miles east and one mile south from Billings, Oklahoma. It’s about 10 miles directly south of Tonkawa and 16 miles north and due west of Perry. Billings is the nearest town to where my family’s farm is located.
implicit_text: Our home place is seven miles east and one mile south from a small town in northern Oklahoma. It’s about 10 miles directly south of the city where I was born and 16 miles north and due west of another nearby town. That small town is the nearest community to where my family’s farm is located.
cues: home place seven miles east and one mile south from Billings
16 miles north and west of Perry

explicit_text: Perry, Oklahoma, is about 16 miles south and due east from our farm. It’s one of the nearby towns that helps place where we live geographically. While we didn’t live there, it was a known point of reference for us.
implicit_text: A town about 16 miles south and due east from our farm helps place where we live geographically. While we didn’t live there, it was a known point of reference for us in northern Oklahoma.
cues: 16 miles north and due west of Perry

explicit_text: My father was born in Kansas, near Pittsburgh, where his dad mined coal. The family moved from Kansas to what was then no-man’s land, now Beaver County, Oklahoma. After some years pioneering there, they returned to Kansas before my father came back to Oklahoma to claim land after the Cherokee Strip opening.
implicit_text: My father was born in a Midwestern state, near a city known for its coal mining, where his dad worked underground. The family moved from that state to what was then no-man’s land, now part of western Oklahoma. After some years pioneering there, they returned to that state before my father came back to Oklahoma to claim land after the Cherokee Strip opening.

explicit_text: My father’s family lived near Pittsburgh, Kansas, where his father worked mining coal in a strip mine using horses. It was from there that they made the journey westward to no-man’s land in Oklahoma. Pittsburgh was the starting point for their pioneering adventure.
implicit_text: My father’s family lived near a coal mining city in southeastern Kansas, where his father worked mining coal in a strip mine using horses. It was from there that they made the journey westward to no-man’s land in Oklahoma. That city was the starting point for their pioneering adventure.
cues: starting point before moving to no-man’s land

explicit_text: My father’s family lived in Beaver County, Oklahoma, which was then known as no-man’s land. They survived for about five years living in a dugout on the bank of the Beaver River. It was a tough life with no trees and harsh winters, but they made a living working for ranchers and hauling buffalo bones.
implicit_text: My father’s family lived in a western Oklahoma county, which was then known as no-man’s land. They survived for about five years living in a dugout on the bank of a river sharing the county’s name. It was a tough life with no trees and harsh winters, but they made a living working for ranchers and hauling buffalo bones.
cues: lived in no-man’s land now Beaver County

explicit_text: The dugout my father’s family lived in was right on the bank of the Beaver River. They lived there year-round despite the cold and lack of building materials. The riverbank was their home during those pioneering years in no-man’s land.
implicit_text: The dugout my father’s family lived in was right on the bank of a river named after the county where they lived. They lived there year-round despite the cold and lack of building materials. The riverbank was their home during those pioneering years in no-man’s land.
cues: dugout on bank of Beaver River

explicit_text: The Cherokee Strip opened in 1893, but my father was too young to participate in the initial run. However, in 1897 he bought another man’s rights to a claim and settled on the plains near where we live today. This event was pivotal in establishing our family’s land here.
implicit_text: The land opening in northern Oklahoma in 1893 was called the Cherokee Strip, but my father was too young to participate in the initial run. However, in 1897 he bought another man’s rights to a claim and settled on the plains near where we live today. This event was pivotal in establishing our family’s land here.
cues: settled on plains near current home

explicit_text: When my father’s family moved from Pittsburgh, Kansas to no-man’s land, they traveled with a covered wagon and a small herd of cattle. They passed right through Wichita, which was barely more than a village at the time, driving their cattle down the main street. It was quite a journey for them.
implicit_text: When my father’s family moved from southeastern Kansas to no-man’s land, they traveled with a covered wagon and a small herd of cattle. They passed right through a growing settlement in south-central Kansas, which was barely more than a village at the time, driving their cattle down the main street. It was quite a journey for them.
cues: Wichita was a small village then

explicit_text: My mother, Edith, was from Iowa, where her family farmed near Clarinda. They moved to Oklahoma in 1907, traveling in two covered wagons over about three weeks. The trip was made pleasant by fishing and swimming along the way, and my mother walked much of the distance.
implicit_text: My mother, Edith, was from a Midwestern state where her family farmed near a small town in the southwest corner. They moved to Oklahoma in 1907, traveling in two covered wagons over about three weeks. The trip was made pleasant by fishing and swimming along the way, and my mother walked much of the distance.

explicit_text: My mother’s family farmed near Clarinda, Iowa before moving to Oklahoma. It was from there that they began their journey westward in 1907. Clarinda represents the roots of my mother’s side of the family.

explicit_text: My father, George Delbert Bellmon, was born in 1874 in Kansas and was a hard-working man despite limited formal education. He lived through pioneering years in no-man’s land, survived tough conditions, and later became an oil field contractor. He valued education highly and made sure all his children received schooling, even through the Depression.

explicit_text: One of my brothers earned a degree from Oklahoma State University, which shows how my father’s insistence on education paid off. Despite financial hardships during the Depression, our family managed to get a college education. OSU was an important institution for us.

explicit_text: The Centennial Farm is across the road from where we live now. The original house there was built around 1898, and it was where I did many chores growing up. It’s a significant family place that connects us to our farming heritage.
